name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يُوصِيكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَوْلَـٰدِكُمْ ۖ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۚ فَإِن كُنَّ نِسَآءً فَوْقَ ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثَا مَا تَرَكَ ۖ وَإِن كَانَتْ وَٰحِدَةً فَلَهَا ٱلنِّصْفُ ۚ وَلِأَبَوَيْهِ لِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ مِمَّا تَرَكَ إِن كَانَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَوَرِثَهُۥٓ أَبَوَاهُ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلثُّلُثُ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُۥٓ إِخْوَةٌ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِى بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ لَا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعًا ۚ فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١١)
```

```english
Allah enjoins you concerning your children: for the male shall be the like of the share of two females, and if there be [two or] more than two females, then for them shall be two-thirds of what he leaves; but if she be alone, then for her shall be a half; and for each of his parents a sixth of what he leaves, if he has children; but if he has no children, and his parents are his [sole] heirs, then it shall be a third for his mother; but if he has brothers, then a sixth for his mother, after [paying off] any bequest he may have made or any debt [he may have incurred]. Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is likelier to be beneficial for you. This is an ordinance from Allah. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (11)
```

```arabic
۞ وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ أَزْوَٰجُكُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُنَّ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُنَّ وَلَدٌ فَلَكُمُ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْنَ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِينَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۚ وَلَهُنَّ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّكُمْ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌ فَلَهُنَّ ٱلثُّمُنُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُم ۚ مِّنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ تُوصُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ وَإِن كَانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلَـٰلَةً أَوِ ٱمْرَأَةٌ وَلَهُۥٓ أَخٌ أَوْ أُخْتٌ فَلِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ فَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فِى ٱلثُّلُثِ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصَىٰ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ غَيْرَ مُضَآرٍّ ۚ وَصِيَّةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٢)
```

```english
For you shall be a half of what your wives leave, if they have no children; but if they have children, then for you shall be a fourth of what they leave, after [paying off] any bequest they may have made or any debt [they may have incurred]. And for them [it shall be] a fourth of what you leave, if you have no children; but if you have children, then for them shall be an eighth of what you leave, after [paying off] any bequest you may have made or any debt [you may have incurred]. If a man or woman is inherited by siblings and has a brother or a sister, then each of them shall receive a sixth; but if they are more than that, then they shall share in one third, after [paying off] any bequest he may have made or any debt [he may have incurred] without prejudice. [This is] an enjoinment from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-forbearing. (12)
```

```arabic
تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٣)
```

```english
These are Allah’s bounds, and whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He shall admit him to gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success. (13)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ نَارًا خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (١٤)
```

```english
But whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle and transgresses the bounds set by Allah, He shall make him enter a Fire, to remain in it [forever], and there will be a humiliating punishment for him. (14)
```

## (Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: **يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** — **Allah enjoins you concerning your children: for the male shall be the like of the share of two females** (4:11). **Enjoining** (الإيصاء) and **recommending** (التوصية) refer to a covenant (العهد) and a command (الأمر). Al-Raghib stated in *Mufradat al-Quran*: "**The bequest** (الوصية) is to direct another toward that which they should act upon, coupled with an exhortation (وعظ)." End quote.

The shift from the term "sons" (الأبناء) to "children" (الأولاد) indicates that the ruling of one share and two shares is specific to the direct offspring of the deceased without an intermediary. As for the children of children and those below them, their ruling is the ruling of the person through whom they are connected. Thus, the daughter of a son receives two shares and the son of a daughter receives one share if there is no one in a prior rank to precede them, just as the ruling for the children of brothers and sisters is the ruling of the person through whom they are connected. As for the term "son" (الابن), it does not necessarily negate an intermediary, just as "father" (الأب) is more general than "progenitor" (الوالد).

Regarding His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: **آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ لا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً** — **Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is closer to you in usefulness** (4:11), it will be explained later that there is a specific rhetorical purpose (عناية خاصة) necessitating the choice of the term "sons" (الأبناء) over "children" (الأولاد).

As for His saying: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** — **for the male shall be the like of the share of two females**, the selection of this expression contains an allusion to the invalidation of the practice of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) regarding the prohibition of women from inheriting. It is as if He made the inheritance of the female an established and known matter and then informed that the male receives twice her share; or He made her share the primary basis in legislation (التشريع) and made the male's inheritance dependent upon it, known by relation to it. Had it not been for this, He would have said: "for the female is half the share of the male," but then it would not convey this meaning, nor would the context harmonize with it—as you can see. This is what some scholars have mentioned, and it is sound. This is perhaps supported by the fact that the verse does not explicitly and independently address any shares except those of women; even when it mentions the shares of men, it is in conjunction with the mention of their shares, as in the following verse and the verse at the end of the Surah.

In short, His saying: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females) serves as an explanation (تفسير) for His saying: **يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ** (Allah enjoins you concerning your children). The definite article (اللام) in "the male" (الذكر) and "the two females" (الأنثيين) is for defining the genus (تعريف الجنس); meaning that the genus of the male is equivalent in share to two females. This only occurs when there are both male and female heirs together, so the male receives twice the share of the female. He did not say: "for the male is like the two shares of the female" or "twice the share of the female," so that the speech might indicate the share of two females when they are alone, preferring conciseness (الإيجاز), as will be explained.

In any case, if the heirs consist of males and females, each male receives two shares and each female receives one share, regardless of their number.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ كُنَّ نِساءً فَوْقَ اثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثا ما تَرَكَ** — **But if they be women, more than two, then for them is two-thirds of what he leaves** (4:11). The appearance of this statement after His saying: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females) indicates that it is based on a suppressed (محذوف) conjunctive clause. It is as if it were said: "This is if they are women and men; but if they be women..." etc. This is common in usage, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَتِمُّوا الْحَجَّ وَالْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ فَإِنْ أُحْصِرْتُمْ فَمَا اسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ الْهَدْيِ** — **Complete the hajj and the ‘umrah for Allah’s sake, and if you are prevented, then [offer] such sacrificial animal as may be feasible** (2:196), and His saying: **أَيَّاماً مَعْدُوداتٍ فَمَنْ كانَ مِنْكُمْ مَرِيضاً أَوْ عَلى سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ** — **[Fasting is for] a certain number of days. But if any of you is ill or on a journey, then [let it be] a [similar] number of other days** (2:184).

The pronoun in "if they be" (كن) refers to the "children" (الأولاد) in His saying: **فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ** (concerning your children). The feminization of the pronoun is due to the feminization of the predicate (الخبر). The pronoun in His saying: **تَرَكَ** (he leaves) refers to the deceased (الميت), known from the context of the speech.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ كانَتْ واحِدَةً فَلَهَا النِّصْفُ** — **and if she be one, then for her is the half** (4:11). The pronoun refers to the "child" (الولد) understood from the context, and its feminization is in consideration of the predicate. What is meant by "the half" (النصف) is half of what he leaves; thus, the definite article (اللام) replaces the possessive construction (المضاف إليه).

He did not mention the share of two females because it is understood from His saying: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females). For if a male and a female are together, the share of the female is one-third according to the verse, and the share of the male is two-thirds, which is the share of two females. Thus, the share of two females is two-thirds. This amount is understood from the speech in general (إجمالا), though it is not inherently definitive for understanding, as it would not contradict a case where it might have been said afterward: "and if they be two, then for them is the half or the whole," for example. However, it is determined by the silence regarding this share and the explicit statement in His saying: "But if they be women, more than two," for it suggests an intentionality in omitting the mention of the share of two females.

Furthermore, the fact that their share is two-thirds is what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله acted upon, and the practice has continued from his time صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله until our time among the scholars of the Ummah, except for what has been narrated of the disagreement of Ibn Abbas.

This is the best of the views in justifying the omission of an explicit mention of the share of two females. Al-Kulayni (may Allah have mercy on him) said in *al-Kafi*: "Allah made the share of two females two-thirds by His saying: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females). This is because if a man leaves a daughter and a son, the male receives the like of the share of two females, which is two-thirds; thus, the share of two females is two-thirds. He deemed this explanation sufficient instead of mentioning two females with two-thirds." End quote. Something similar was narrated from Abu Muslim the exegete: "It is derived from His saying, the Exalted: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females), because a male with one female inherits two-thirds, so two-thirds is the share of two females." End quote. Although what is narrated from them is not without deficiency and requires completion by what we have clarified just now, so let it be reflected upon.

There are other weak (سخيفة) views mentioned in justifying the verse, such as the saying of some: that what is meant by His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ كُنَّ نِساءً فَوْقَ اثْنَتَيْنِ** (But if they be women, more than two) is two and what is above them; thus, this sentence includes the explanation of the share of two females and women above two together. And like the saying of some: that the ruling for two daughters here is known by analogy (القياس) to the ruling for two sisters in the last verse of the Surah, where two-thirds was mentioned for them, among other things from which His speech, the Exalted, is far removed.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِأَبَوَيْهِ لِكُلِّ واحِدٍ مِنْهُمَا السُّدُسُ** — **And to his parents, to each of them a sixth** (4:11) until His saying: **فَلِأُمِّهِ السُّدُسُ** — **then for his mother is a sixth** (4:11). The conjunction of the parents in the ruling with the children indicates that the parents share with the children in their rank (طبقتهم). His saying: **وَوَرِثَهُ أَبَواهُ** — **and his parents inherit from him** (4:11) means the heirs are restricted to them. In His saying: **فَإِنْ كانَ لَهُ إِخْوَةٌ** — **but if he has brothers** (4:11) after His saying: **فَإِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلَدٌ وَوَرِثَهُ أَبَواهُ** — **but if he has no child and his parents inherit from him** (4:11), there is an indication that brothers are situated in a second rank (طبقة ثانية) subsequent to the rank of sons and daughters; they do not inherit in their presence, except that the brothers exclude (تحجب) the mother from the third.

His saying, the Exalted: **مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِي بِها أَوْ دَيْنٍ** — **after [paying] any bequest he may have bequeathed or any debt** (4:11). As for the bequest (الوصية), it is that which is recommended by His saying: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ إِنْ تَرَكَ خَيْراً الْوَصِيَّةُ** — **Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and if he leaves any property, is that he should make a bequest** (2:180). Its precedence in the verse over the debt (الدين) does not contradict what has been established in the Sunnah (السنة) — *the prophetic tradition and practice* — that the debt takes precedence over the bequest. This is because in speech, the less important may sometimes be mentioned before the more important, because the more important, due to its status and the strength of its establishment, may not require the same level of emphasis (التأكيد) and stress (التشديد) that others require, of which precedence is a form. Accordingly, His saying: **أَوْ دَيْنٍ** (or any debt) is in the position of digression (الإضراب) and natural progression (الترقي).

By this, the reason for describing the bequest with His saying: "he may have bequeathed" (يوصي بها) becomes clear, as it indicates emphasis. Furthermore, it is not devoid of an allusion to the necessity of honoring the deceased and observing his sanctity in what he has bequeathed, as He, the Exalted, said: **فَمَنْ بَدَّلَهُ بَعْدَ ما سَمِعَهُ فَإِنَّما إِثْمُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُ** — **Then whoever changes it after hearing it, its sin shall rest only on those who change it** (2:181).

His saying, the Exalted: **آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ لا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً** — **Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is closer to you in usefulness** (4:11). The address is to the heirs, meaning the general body of the legally responsible (المكلفين) insofar as they inherit from their deceased. It is a statement delivered to hint at the secret behind the variation of shares in the inheritance of parents and children, and a kind of instruction for them, addressed in the language of "you do not know," and such expressions are common in the language.

Moreover, if the address were to those other than the heirs—meaning people from the perspective that they will die and be inherited by their parents and children—there would be no reason for His saying: **أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً** (closer to you in usefulness), for it is apparent that what is meant by "usefulness" (الانتفاع) is the benefit from the inherited wealth, which returns to the heirs rather than the deceased.

The precedence of parents over children suggests that parents are closer in usefulness than children, as in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الصَّفا وَالْمَرْوَةَ مِنْ شَعائِرِ اللهِ** — **Indeed Safa and Marwah are among Allah’s sacraments** (2:158), and the narration has already passed from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said: "I begin with what Allah began with."

The matter is thus in view of the effects of kinship (الرحم) and the consideration of human emotions; for a person is more compassionate toward his child than toward his parents, and he sees the survival of his child as the survival of himself, unlike the survival of his parents. Thus, a person's parents are more strongly connected and more closely tied in existence to him than his children. If the inheritance benefit is built upon this principle, its necessary consequence is that a person, when inheriting from his father, for example, should receive a larger share than when inheriting from his son, for example, even if it might initially occur to a primitive mind that the matter should be the opposite.

This verse—meaning His saying: **آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ لا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً** (Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is closer to you in usefulness)—is among the evidences that He, the Exalted, built the ruling of inheritance on an ontological (تكويني) and external basis, like all other Islamic primordial (فطرية) rulings.

Furthermore, the absolute (مطلقة) Quranic verses that look toward the origin of legislation also indicate this, such as His saying: **فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** — **So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion** (30:30). How can it be conceived, in the presence of such verses, that mandatory rulings and unchangeable obligations (فرائض) would appear in the Sharia (الشريعة) — *the divine law* — without having an origin in the ontological order (التكوين) in general?

It may be possible to sense from the verse—meaning His saying: **آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ** (Your parents and your children) etc.—the precedence of the children of children over grandfathers and grandmothers, for grandfathers and grandmothers do not inherit in the presence of children and the children of children.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللهِ** — **[This is] an ordinance from Allah** (4:11) etc. It is apparent that it is in the accusative case (منصوب) due to a suppressed verb, the estimation (التقدير) being "take" or "adhere to" and the like. It is a strong emphasis that these mentioned shares have been presented to you and are apportioned (مفرزة) and determined (معينة), not to be changed from how they were placed.

This verse is responsible for explaining the shares of the first rank (الطبقة الأولى), which are the children, the father, and the mother, in all their possibilities. This is either explicitly (تصريحا), such as the share of the father and mother, which is a sixth for each of them in the presence of children, and a third or a sixth for the mother in their absence as mentioned in the verse; and like the share of a single daughter, which is a half, and the share of daughters if they are alone, which is two-thirds, and the share of sons and daughters if they are together, which is for the male like the share of two females, and the share of two daughters is joined to it as two-thirds, as previously mentioned.

Or it is implicitly (تلويحا), such as the share of a single son, for he inherits the entire wealth due to His saying: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females) and His saying regarding the daughter: **وَإِنْ كانَتْ واحِدَةً فَلَهَا النِّصْفُ** (and if she be one, then for her is the half). Likewise, sons if they are alone, as is understood from His saying: **لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females), that sons are equal in shares. The matter of the verse in its conciseness is wondrous.

Know also that the implication of the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse is the lack of difference in inheriting wealth and the enjoyment of the heirs between the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and other people. A similar instance of this absolute or general nature has already passed in His saying, the Exalted: **لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَالْأَقْرَبُونَ وَلِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ** — **Men have a share in what parents and near relatives leave, and women have a share** (4:7). As for what might be said—that the general addresses of the Quran do not include the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله because they flow from his tongue—it is something that should not be listened to.

Yes, there is a dispute here between the Sunnis and the Shi'a regarding whether the Prophet leaves an inheritance or whether what he left is charity (صدقة), originating from the narration reported by Abu Bakr in the story of Fadak. The discussion of this is outside the scope of this book, and therefore we see the treatment of it here as superfluous; let it be referred to its appropriate place.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ ما تَرَكَ أَزْواجُكُمْ** — **For you is a half of what your wives leave** (4:12) until His saying: **تُوصُونَ بِها أَوْ دَيْنٍ** — **you may have bequeathed or any debt** (4:12). The meaning is clear. The "half" (النصف) has been used in a possessive construction (بالإضافة), so it was said: "half of what she leaves" (نصف ما ترك), while the "quarter" (الربع) was used independently (بالقطع), so it was said: "and for them is the quarter of what you leave" (ولهن الربع مما تركتم). For cutting off from the possessive construction necessitates completion with "from" (من), whether explicit or estimated. This "from" (من) conveys the meaning of taking and beginning from a thing. This meaning is suitable when the object of "from" is like a subordinate part of the thing started from and as if consumed within it. This is only suitable when the object is small or what is like small in relation to the whole, such as the sixth, the quarter, and the third of the total, rather than the like of the half and the two-thirds. Therefore, He, the Exalted, said: **السُّدُسُ مِمَّا تَرَكَ** (the sixth of what he leaves), and said: **فَلِأُمِّهِ الثُّلُثُ** (then for his mother is the third), and said: **فَلَكُمُ الرُّبُعُ** (then for you is the quarter), cutting off from the possessive construction in all of that. And He said: **وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ ما تَرَكَ** (and for you is half of what she leaves), and said: **فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثا ما تَرَكَ** (then for them is two-thirds of what he leaves) with the possessive construction, and said: **فَلَهَا النِّصْفُ** (then for her is the half), meaning half of what he leaves, so the definite article (اللام) replaces the possessive construction.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ كانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلالَةً أَوِ امْرَأَةٌ** — **And if a man or a woman is inherited from as a kalalah** (4:12) until the end of the verse. The root of **kalalah** (الكلالة) is a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning "encompassing" (الإحاطة). From it comes the crown (الإكليل) because it encompasses the head, and from it comes "all" (الكل) — *with a damma on the kaf* — because it encompasses the parts, and from it comes "burden" (الكل) — *with a fatha on the kaf* — for a kind of heavy encompassing upon the one who is burdened by it. Al-Raghib said: "**Kalalah** (الكلالة) is a name for heirs other than the child and the parent." He said: "It is narrated that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was asked about *kalalah*, and he said: 'One who dies having neither child nor parent,' so he made it a name for the deceased. Both sayings are correct, for *kalalah* is a verbal noun that encompasses both the heir and the inherited." End quote.

I say: Accordingly, there is no obstacle to "was" (كان) being incomplete (ناقصة), with "man" (رجل) as its subject (اسمها), "is inherited from" (يورث) as an attribute for the man, and "kalalah" (كلالة) as its predicate (خبرها). The meaning would be: "And if the deceased is a *kalalah* to the heir, being neither his father nor his son." It is also possible for "was" (كان) to be complete (تامة), with "a man who is inherited from" as its subject (فاعله) and "kalalah" as a verbal noun placed in the position of a circumstantial qualifier (الحال). The meaning also reverts to the deceased being a *kalalah* to the heirs. Al-Zajjaj said, as narrated from him: "Whoever reads *yurithu* (يورث) — *with a kasra on the ra* — then *kalalah* is an object (مفعول); and whoever reads *yurathu* (يورث) — *with a fatha on the ra* — then *kalalah* is in the accusative as a circumstantial qualifier."

His saying: **غَيْرَ مُضارٍّ** — **without being harmful** (4:12) is in the accusative as a circumstantial qualifier. **Harmfulness** (المضارة) is to cause harm (الإضرار). Its apparent meaning is that what is intended is harming through debt on the part of the deceased, such as feigning debt to harm the heirs and deprive them of the inheritance; or what is intended is harming through the debt as they mentioned regarding a bequest that exceeds one-third of the wealth.

His saying, the Exalted: **تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ** — **These are Allah’s bounds** (4:13) until the end of the two verses. The **bound** (الحد) is the barrier (الحاجز) between two things that prevents one from mixing with the other and the loss of distinction between them, like the boundary of a house or a garden. What is meant by them are the rulings of inheritance and the explained obligations. Allah has magnified their matter by what He mentioned in the two verses of reward for obeying Him and obeying His Messenger regarding them, and the eternal, humiliating punishment for disobedience.

## (A Discourse on Inheritance in a Universal Manner)

These two verses—meaning His saying, the Exalted: **يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ** (Allah enjoins you concerning your children) until the end of the two verses—and the verse at the end of the Surah: **يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ قُلِ اللهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِي الْكَلالَةِ** — **They ask you for a ruling. Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning the kalalah’** (4:176) until the end of the verse, along with His saying, the Exalted: **لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ** (Men have a share in what parents leave) the verse, and with His saying, the Exalted: **وَأُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ** — **and blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah** (33:6, 8:75)—these five or six verses are the Quranic basis for inheritance in Islam, and the Sunnah explains them with the clearest explanation and detail.

The universals (الكليات) extracted and derived from them, which are the basis for the details of the rulings, are several matters: Among them: what preceded in His saying: **آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ لا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً** (Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is closer to you in usefulness), from which it appears that proximity (القرب) and distance (البعد) from the deceased have an effect in the chapter of inheritance. When this clause is joined to the rest of the verse, it implies that this affects the increase or decrease, and the greatness or smallness of the share. When it is joined to His saying, the Exalted: **وَأُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ** (and blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah), it implies that the closer in lineage (نسبا) in the chapter of inheritance excludes the more distant.

Thus, the closest relatives to the deceased are the father, the mother, the son, and the daughter, as there is no intermediary between them and the deceased. The son and the daughter exclude their own children because they are connected to him through their mediation; so if their mediation is lost, they stand in their place.

This is followed by the second rank (المرتبة الثانية), who are the brothers and sisters of the deceased and his grandfather and grandmother, for they are connected to the deceased through one intermediary, which is the father or the mother. The children of the brother and sister stand in the place of their father and mother, and each generation (بطن) excludes the generation after it, as mentioned.

This rank is followed by the rank of the deceased's paternal uncles, maternal uncles, paternal aunts, and maternal aunts, for between them and the deceased are two intermediaries, which are the grandfather or grandmother and the father or mother. The matter follows the same principle of standing in place as mentioned.

It appears from the mentioned issue of proximity and distance that the one with two connections (ذا السببين) takes precedence over the one with one connection (ذي السبب الواحد). From this is the precedence of the *kalalah* of both parents over the *kalalah* of the father, so the latter does not inherit with the former. As for the *kalalah* of the mother, the *kalalah* of both parents does not crowd it out.

Among them: that in the heirs, precedence and delay have been considered from another aspect. For shares may sometimes combine and thus crowd each other out (تزاحمت) by exceeding the original estate. Among them are those for whom another share has been designated during crowding, such as the husband who takes a half, but if a child crowds him, he reverts to exactly a quarter; and similarly the wife in her quarter and her eighth. And like the mother who takes a third, but if a child or brothers crowd her, she reverts to a sixth. The father does not move from his sixth whether a child exists or not. Among them are those for whom a share was designated, then if another crowds them, the text is silent about them and does not mention a specific share for them, such as the daughter, daughters, sister, and sisters who take the half and the two-thirds; their shares have been passed over in silence during crowding. It is derived from this that those who are prioritized are not crowded out, nor does any reduction (نقص) fall upon them in the case of the shares exceeding the original estate; rather, whatever reduction occurs falls upon the others whose shares were passed over in silence during crowding.

Among them: that the shares may exceed the wealth, such as if a husband and sisters from the *kalalah* of both parents are assumed; there is a half and two-thirds, which is more than the common denominator (مخرج) of the wealth. Likewise, if parents, two daughters, and a husband are assumed, the shares exceed the original estate, for they are two-sixths, two-thirds, and a quarter.

Similarly, the estate may exceed the obligation (الفريضة), such as when there is only one daughter or only two daughters, and so on. The narrated Sunnah, which has the status of explaining the Book according to what has been reported through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), is that in the case of the shares exceeding the original wealth, the reduction enters upon those for whom only one share was designated, namely the daughters and sisters, and not others, such as the mother and the spouses whose obligations Allah designated according to the change of circumstances. Likewise, in the case of the original estate exceeding the shares, the surplus is returned to those upon whom the reduction would enter in the previous case, as in a daughter and a father; the father has a sixth and the daughter has half the wealth by obligation, and the remainder is by return (الرد).

Umar ibn al-Khattab, during the days of his caliphate, instituted in the case of the shares exceeding the wealth the practice of *awl* (العول) — *the proportional reduction of all shares* — and people in the early period acted in the case of the estate exceeding the shares by *tasib* (التعصيب) — *the distribution of the residue to male agnates*. The discussion on both will come in the upcoming traditional research, Allah willing.

Among them: that reflection on the shares of men and women in inheritance implies that the woman's share is less than the man's share in general, except in the case of the parents, for the mother's share may exceed the father's share according to the obligation. Perhaps the favoring of the mother's side over the father's side or their equalization is because she is, in Islam, more closely related (أمس رحما) to her child and endures every hardship in carrying, delivering, nursing, and raising him. He, the Exalted, said: **وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ إِحْساناً حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُ كُرْهاً وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهاً وَحَمْلُهُ وَفِصالُهُ ثَلاثُونَ شَهْراً** — **We have enjoined man to be kind to his parents. His mother has carried him with hardship and delivered him with hardship, and his bearing and weaning takes thirty months** (46:15). The departure of her share from half of what is for the man to the point of equality or increase is certainly a favoring of her side.

As for the man's share being, in general, twice the woman's share, it has considered the man's superiority (فضل) over the woman according to the management of life by reason (عقلا) and the fact that the necessary maintenance (الإنفاق) is his responsibility. He, the Exalted, said: **الرِّجالُ قَوَّامُونَ عَلَى النِّساءِ بِما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ وَبِما أَنْفَقُوا مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ** — **Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others, and by virtue of their spending of their wealth** (4:34). **Manager** (القوام) is from "standing" (القيام), which is the management of livelihood. What is meant by **advantage** (الفضل) is the increase in rationality (التعقل), for his life is a rational life while the woman's life is an emotional and sentimental (إحساسية عاطفية) life. Giving the reins of wealth to a rational, managing hand is closer to rectitude than giving it to a hand possessed of emotional sentiment. This giving and specification, when measured against the existing wealth in the world transferred from the present generation to the next, results in the management of two-thirds of the existing wealth being with men and the management of one-third with women. Thus, the management of reason prevails over the management of sentiment and emotions, so the affairs of society are rectified and life becomes happy.

This reduction occurring to women has been compensated for by what Allah, the Glorified, commanded the man regarding justice in her affair, which necessitates her partnership with the man in what is in his hand of the two-thirds; thus, the woman takes half of these two-thirds in terms of consumption (المصرف). And she has the one-third which she owns and over which she has the authority of ownership and consumption.

The result of this wondrous arrangement and legislation is that the man and the woman are opposites in ownership and consumption. The man owns two-thirds of the world's wealth and has the consumption of one-third of it, while the woman owns one-third of the wealth and has the consumption of two-thirds of it. In this, the prevalence of the spirit of reason over the spirit of sentiment and emotions in the man has been observed; for financial management through preservation, exchange, production, and profit-seeking is more suitable and closer to the spirit of reason. And the prevalence of delicate emotions and subtle sentiments over the spirit of reason in the woman has been observed, which is more suitable and closer to consumption. This is the secret in the difference that Islam has considered in the chapter of inheritance and maintenance between men and women.

It is appropriate that the natural increase in the spirit of reason in the man and his distinction over the woman in this regard is what is meant by the "advantage" (الفضل) mentioned by Allah, the Glorified, in His saying, Exalted is He: **الرِّجالُ قَوَّامُونَ عَلَى النِّساءِ بِما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ** (Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others), rather than an increase in might, severity, and hardness. For although coarseness and roughness in the genus of men is an existential distinction by which the man is distinguished from the woman, and upon which great effects follow in human society in the chapters of defense, preservation, arduous labor, enduring hardships and trials, and steadfastness and tranquility in tremors and terrors—and these are necessary matters in life that the genus of women cannot naturally fulfill—

However, women are also equipped with what corresponds to it of subtle sentiments and delicate emotions that society cannot do without in its life. These have important effects in the chapters of intimacy, love, tranquility, mercy, compassion, and enduring the burdens of procreation, pregnancy, delivery, nursing, upbringing, nursing the sick, and serving the homes. The state of man is not rectified by roughness and coarseness were it not for softness and delicacy, nor by anger were it not for desire, nor the affairs of the world by repulsion were it not for attraction.

In short, these are two balanced equipments in the man and the woman by which the scales of life are balanced in the mixed society composed of both genera. Far be it from Him, the Glorified, to be unfair in His speech or unjust in His ruling: **أَمْ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يَحِيفَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ** — **Or do they fear that Allah will be unfair to them?** (24:50), **وَلا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً** — **and your Lord does not wrong anyone** (18:49), and He is the one who says: **بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ** — **you are all [members] of the same [human] kind** (3:195). He has pointed to this harmony and partiality by His saying in the verse: **بِما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ** (because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others).

He also said: **وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ إِذا أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ تَنْتَشِرُونَ وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَرَحْمَةً إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ** — **Among His signs is that He created you from dust, and behold, you are human beings spreading [all over]! And of His signs is that He created for you mates from your own selves that you may take comfort in them, and He ordained affection and mercy between you. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect** (30:20-21). Look at the wondrous explanation of the two verses, where He described the human (who is the man by the evidence of the contrast) with "spreading" (الانتشار), which is the striving in seeking livelihood. To this return all acts of acquiring the necessities of life by resorting to power and severity, even what is in competitions, battles, and raids. If the human had only this spreading, his individuals would be divided into one who attacks and another who flees.

But Allah, the Glorified, created women and equipped them with what necessitates that men find comfort in them, and He placed between them affection and mercy; so they attracted men with beauty, coquetry, affection, and mercy. Thus, women are the first pillar and the essential factor for human society.

From here, Islam made the domestic society, which is marriage, the basis in this chapter. He, the Exalted, said: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ** — **O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you** (49:13). So He began with the matter of the marriage of the male and the female and the appearance of procreation through that, then built upon it the large society composed of nations and tribes.

From the end of the verse, it appears that the mentioned advantage in His saying: **الرِّجالُ قَوَّامُونَ عَلَى النِّساءِ بِما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ** (Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others) is only an advantage in equipment by which the affairs of worldly life—meaning livelihood—are organized in the best organization, and the state of society is well rectified. It is not intended to mean nobility (الكرامة), which is the true virtue in Islam, namely proximity and closeness to Allah, the Glorified. For Islam does not care for any of the physical increases that are only utilized for material life; rather, they are means used for what is with Allah.

It has been concluded from all that we have presented that men were favored over women with the spirit of reason which necessitated a difference in the matter of inheritance and its like, but it is a virtue in the sense of increase. As for virtue in the sense of nobility which Islam cares for, it is godwariness (التقوى) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — wherever it may be.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi—in his *Sunan* through the paths of Jabir ibn Abdillah—who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and Abu Bakr visited me in Bani Salimah, walking. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله found me unconscious, so he called for water and performed ablution with it, then sprinkled it over me. I regained consciousness and said: 'What do you command me to do with my wealth, O Messenger of Allah?' Then it was revealed: **يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** (Allah enjoins you concerning your children: for the male shall be the like of the share of two females)."

I say: It has been mentioned repeatedly that the narrated occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) do not preclude being multiple and several of them gathering for one verse, nor do they contradict the fact that the concern of the revealed verse is not restricted to them, nor that the revelation might coincide so that the content of the verse applies to them. Thus, the narration is not harmed by Jabir's saying: "What do you command me to do with my wealth, O Messenger of Allah? Then it was revealed..." etc., even though the division of wealth was not his responsibility such that it would be answered by the verse. More wondrous than it is what was also narrated from Abd ibn Humayd and al-Hakim from Jabir, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to visit me while I was ill. I said: 'How should I divide my wealth among my children?' He did not return any answer to me, and then it was revealed: **يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ** (Allah enjoins you concerning your children)."

In the same source, it is narrated by Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim from al-Suddi, who said: "The people of the Age of Ignorance did not give inheritance to girls, nor to the weak among boys. A man would not inherit from his father except one who was capable of fighting. Then Abd al-Rahman, the brother of Hassan the poet, died and left a wife called Umm Kuhhah and five daughters. The heirs came and took his wealth. Umm Kuhhah complained of that to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so Allah revealed this verse: **فَإِنْ كُنَّ نِساءً فَوْقَ اثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثا ما تَرَكَ**—**وَإِنْ كانَتْ واحِدَةً فَلَهَا النِّصْفُ** (But if they be women, more than two, then for them is two-thirds of what he leaves—and if she be one, then for her is the half). Then He said regarding Umm Kuhhah: **وَلَهُنَّ الرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ إِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌ**—**فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌ فَلَهُنَّ الثُّمُنُ** (And for them is a fourth of what you leave if you have no child—but if you have a child, then for them is an eighth)."

In the same source, also from them from Ibn Abbas, who said: "When the verse of obligations (آية الفرائض) was revealed—in which Allah ordained what He ordained for the male and female child and the parents—the people, or some of them, disliked it and said: 'A woman is given a quarter or an eighth, and a daughter is given a half, and a small boy is given, yet none of these fight the enemy nor acquire booty.' They used to do that in the Age of Ignorance—they would not give inheritance except to those who fought the enemy, and they would give it to the eldest and then the next eldest."

I say: Among it was **tasib** (التعصيب), which is giving the inheritance to the paternal agnates (عصبة الأب) if the deceased did not leave an adult son capable of fighting. The Sunnis have acted upon it regarding the surplus over the obligation in cases where the shares do not exhaust the estate. Something of this may be found in their narrations, but narrations have come through the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) negating *tasib*, and that the surplus over the obligations is returned to those upon whom the reduction enters, namely the children and the brothers from both parents or the father, and to the father in some cases. What is derived from the verses agrees with that, as mentioned.

In the same source, it is narrated by al-Hakim and al-Bayhaqi from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The first to apply *awl* (أعال) to the obligations was Umar. They crowded upon him and some rode over others. He said: 'By Allah, I do not know what to do with you! By Allah, I do not know which of you Allah put forward and which He put back? I find nothing better in this wealth than to divide it among you by proportions (بالحصص)!' Then Ibn Abbas said: 'By Allah—had he put forward whom Allah put forward and put back whom Allah put back, no obligation would have undergone *awl*.' It was said to him: 'And which did Allah put forward?' He said: 'Every obligation that Allah did not lower from an obligation except to another obligation—this is what Allah put forward. And every obligation that, when it departs from its share, has nothing but what remains—that is what Allah put back. So that which He put forward is like the two spouses and the mother, and that which He put back is like the sisters and daughters. So when those whom Allah put forward and those He put back gather, one begins with those He put forward and gives them their full right; then if anything remains, it is for them [the others], and if nothing remains, there is nothing for them.'"

In the same source, also narrated by Sa'id ibn Mansur from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Do you think that He who counted the sands of Alij in number—made in the wealth a half, a third, and a quarter? It is only two halves, three thirds, and four quarters."

In the same source, also from him from Ata, who said: "I said to Ibn Abbas: 'The people do not take my word nor your word—and if I and you were to die, they would not divide an inheritance according to what you say.' He said: 'Let them gather, and let us place our hands on the Corner (الركن)—then let us invoke (نبتهل) and place the curse of Allah upon the liars: Allah did not judge according to what they said.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated from Ibn Abbas through the paths of the Shi'a as well, as will come.

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Zuhri from Ubaydullah ibn Abdillah ibn Utbah, who said: "I sat with Ibn Abbas and the mention of the obligations of inheritance was brought up. Ibn Abbas said: 'Glory be to Allah the Great—do you think that He who counted the sands of Alij in number made in a wealth a half, a half, and a third?! These two halves have taken the wealth, so where is the place for the third?' Zufar ibn Aws al-Basri said to him: 'O Aba al-Abbas, who was the first to apply *awl* to these obligations?' He said: 'Umar ibn al-Khattab. When the obligations became entangled before him and some pushed others, he said: "By Allah, I do not know which of you Allah put forward and which He put back? I find nothing more spacious than to divide this wealth among you by proportions and enter upon every possessor of a right his right." So he entered upon it the *awl* of obligations. By Allah, had he put forward whom Allah put forward and put back whom Allah put back, the obligation would not have undergone *awl*.' Zufar ibn Aws said to him: 'And which did He put forward and which did He put back?' He said: 'Every obligation that Allah did not lower from an obligation except to another obligation—this is what Allah put forward. As for what Allah put back—every obligation that, when it departs from its share, has nothing but what remains—that is what He put back. As for those He put forward, the husband has the half—so when what removes him from it enters upon him, he reverts to the quarter, and nothing removes him from it. The wife has the quarter—so when she departs to the eighth, nothing removes her from it. The mother has the third—so when she departs from it, she becomes a sixth, and nothing removes her from it. These are the obligations that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, put forward. As for those He put back, it is the obligation of daughters and sisters; they have the half and the two-thirds—so when the obligations remove them from that, they have nothing but what remains. That is what Allah put back. So when what Allah put forward and what He put back gather, one begins with what Allah put forward—giving its full right—then if anything remains, it is for the one who was put back, and if nothing remains, he has nothing.' Zufar said to him: 'What prevented you from suggesting this view to Umar?' He said: 'Awe of him.'"

I say: This saying of Ibn Abbas was preceded by the saying of Ali (peace be upon him) negating *awl*, and it is the school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), as will come.

In *al-Kafi*, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) used to say: 'Indeed, He who counted the sands of Alij—surely knows that the shares do not undergo *awl* beyond six. If you were to see its face, it would not exceed six.'"

I say: In *al-Sihah*: "**Alij** (عالج) is a place in the desert with sand." His saying (peace be upon him): "the shares do not undergo *awl* beyond six" means they do not deviate from the six so as to change them to something else. The six are the shares explicitly mentioned in the Book, which are: the half, the third, the two-thirds, the quarter, the sixth, and the eighth.

In the same source, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: 'Praise be to Allah, for whom there is no bringer forward of what He put back, nor a putter back of what He brought forward.' Then he struck one of his hands against the other, then said: 'O Ummah that is bewildered after its Prophet—had you put forward whom Allah put forward and put back whom Allah put back, and placed the guardianship (الولاية) and the inheritance where Allah placed them, the friend of Allah would not have been in need, nor would a share of Allah's obligations have undergone *awl*, nor would two have differed in the judgment of Allah, nor would the Ummah have disputed in anything of Allah's affair—except that Ali would have its knowledge from the Book of Allah. So taste the evil consequence of your affair and what you neglected in what your hands sent forward, and Allah is not unjust to the servants, and those who have wronged will soon know to what kind of return they will be returned.'"

I say: The clarification of the reduction entering upon the portions of the heirs, in addition to what has passed, is that the mentioned obligations in His speech, the Exalted, are six: the half, the two-thirds, the third, the sixth, the quarter, and the eighth. These shares may combine with one another such that crowding occurs, just as the half, two-sixths, and a quarter may combine in the first rank, such as a daughter, a father, a mother, and a husband; so the shares exceed the original. Likewise, the two-thirds, two-sixths, and a quarter, such as two daughters, parents, and a husband; so they crowd each other. Similarly, the half, the third, the quarter, and the sixth combine in the second rank, such as a sister, two grandfathers for the father and mother, and a wife. Likewise, the two-thirds, the third, the quarter, and the sixth, such as two sisters, two grandfathers, and a husband.

If we were to enter the reduction upon all the shares, it would be *awl*. But if we preserve the obligation of the parents, the two spouses, and the *kalalah* of the mother—which are the third, the sixth, the half, the quarter, and the eighth—from the entry of reduction upon them—because Allah designated these shares and did not leave them vague in any state, unlike the shares of a single daughter and more, and a single sister for both parents or for the father and more, and unlike the shares of the male and female in cases of singularity and plurality—the reduction always falls upon the children, brothers, and sisters for what has passed.

As for the method of return (الرد), let it be referred to in the collections of hadith and books of jurisprudence.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by al-Hakim and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* from Zayd ibn Thabit: that he used to exclude (يحجب) the mother with two brothers. They said to him: "O Aba Sa'id, Allah says: **فَإِنْ كانَ لَهُ إِخْوَةٌ** (but if he has brothers)—and you exclude her with two brothers?" He said: "Indeed, the Arabs call two brothers 'brothers' (إخوة)."

I say: This is what is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), even though it is well known that **brothers** (الإخوة) is the plural of brother (الأخ), and the plural is not applied to what is less than three.

In *al-Kafi*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "Nothing excludes the mother from the third—except two brothers or four sisters for a father and mother or for a father."

I say: The reports regarding that are many. As for brothers from the mother, they are connected through the mother, and she, by her presence, excludes them. In the reports of both groups, the brothers exclude the mother but do not inherit due to the presence of those who precede them in inheritance, namely the parents. Thus, the brothers' exclusion of the mother despite their not inheriting is only a kind of consideration for the state of the father in terms of returning the surplus over the obligation to him. From this, the reason for the brothers not excluding the mother [in other cases] is known, for they are not dependents of the father.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِي بِها أَوْ دَيْنٍ** (after [paying] any bequest he may have bequeathed or any debt), from Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "You read in this verse the bequest before the debt, but the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله judged with the debt before the bequest."

I say: Al-Suyuti narrated it in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several of the masters of collections and commentaries.

In *al-Kafi*, regarding the meaning of *kalalah* from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "One who is neither a parent nor a child."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ كانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلالَةً** (And if a man is inherited from as a kalalah) the verse: "He only meant by that the brothers and sisters from the mother specifically."

I say: The reports regarding that are many and have been narrated by the Sunnis. The narrations have become widespread (استفاضت) regarding that, and that the ruling for the *kalalah* of the father and both parents is what is mentioned in the concluding verse of the Surah: **يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ قُلِ اللهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِي الْكَلالَةِ** (They ask you for a ruling. Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning the kalalah’) the verse.

Among the evidences for that is that the mentioned obligations for the *kalalah* at the end of the Surah exceed what was mentioned for them in this verse by double or more. It is derived from the context of the verses and the mention of obligations that He, the Exalted, favors the share of men over women in general, favoring the like of two over one or what is close to that whenever possible. The *kalalah* only connects to the deceased from the side of the mother and father or one of them; so the difference observed on the side of the father and mother spreads to them. Thus, the obligations of the *kalalah* of both parents or the father necessarily prevail over the *kalalah* of the mother, and it is thereby revealed that the small amount is for the *kalalah* of the mother and the large amount is for others.

In *al-Ma'ani*, with his chain to Muhammad ibn Sinan: that Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) wrote to him among what he wrote in answer to his questions—the reason for giving women half of what is given to men of inheritance: "Because the woman, when she marries, takes, and the man gives; therefore, it was made abundant for men. And another reason in giving the male twice what is given to the female—because the female is among the dependents of the male if she is in need, and it is upon him to support her and upon him is her maintenance, while it is not upon the woman to support the man, nor is she taken for his maintenance if he is in need. So it was made abundant for men for that, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **الرِّجالُ قَوَّامُونَ عَلَى النِّساءِ**—**بِما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ وَبِما أَنْفَقُوا مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ** (Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others, and by virtue of their spending of their wealth)."

In *al-Kafi*, with his chain to al-Ahwal, who said: "Ibn Abi al-Awja' said: 'What is the matter with the poor, weak woman taking one share while the men take two shares?' Some of our companions mentioned that to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), and he said: 'Indeed, the woman does not have upon her jihad, nor maintenance, nor blood money (معقلة); that is only upon the men. Therefore, the woman was given one share and the man two shares.'"

I say: The narrations with this meaning are many, and the indication of the Book has already passed regarding that.

## (A Scholarly Discourse in Sections)

**1 - The Emergence of Inheritance:** Inheritance—meaning the ownership by some of the living of the wealth left by the deceased—was among the oldest established customs (السنن) circulating in human society. It has gone beyond the capacity of the histories of nations and creeds in our hands to obtain the beginning of its occurrence. It is also from the nature of the matter; for we know by reflecting on the social nature of man that wealth, especially if it is something over which there is no hand, is something man yearns for and his soul longs for to spend on his needs. Its acquisition, especially when there is no obstacle to it, is among his primary, ancient habits. Man, in what he has formed of his society, whether primitive or civilized, cannot do without considering proximity (القرب) and guardianship (الولاية) — *which produce closer proximity and priority* — among the individuals of society, the consideration upon which the formation of the house, the generation, the clan, the tribe, and the like is based. Thus, there is no escape in society from some individuals being more entitled to others, like the child to his parents, the relative to his kin, the friend to his friend, the master to his slave, and one of the spouses to the other, and the leader to his subordinate, even the strong to the weak, although societies differed in identifying that with a severe difference that almost escapes the hand of recording.

The necessity of these two matters is that inheritance has been circulating among them since the oldest social eras.

**2 - The Gradual Transformation of Inheritance:** This custom has not ceased, like other customs circulating in human societies, to transform from state to state, played with by the hand of evolution and perfection since its first appearance. However, because primitive nations did not settle on an organized state, it was difficult to obtain in their histories its organized transformation in a way that provides confidence in it.

The certain amount regarding them is that they used to deprive women and the weak of inheritance; it was only restricted to the strong. This was only because they used to treat women and the weak among slaves and children with the treatment of subjugated animals and goods and commodities that have nothing but to be used by man, without them benefiting from man and what is in his hand or benefiting from social rights that do not exceed the human kind.

Despite that, the referent (مصداق) of the "strong" in this chapter used to differ period after period. Sometimes its referent was the head of the sect or the clan, sometimes the head of the house, and at other times the bravest of the people and the most severe in might. This naturally necessitated a fundamental change in the custom of inheritance.

Because these circulating customs do not guarantee what the human primordial nature (الفطرة) proposes of the proposed happiness, change and substitution would hasten to them. Even the civilized nations among whom laws or what functions like them of customary national traditions used to judge, such as the Romans and the Greeks, were in that state. No law among the laws of inheritance circulating among nations has lasted until today like the Islamic custom of inheritance has lasted; for it has judged among the Islamic nations since its first appearance until today for nearly fourteen centuries.

**3 - Inheritance Among Civilized Nations:** Among the characteristics of the Romans was that they used to see for the house in itself a civil independence that separated it from the general society and protected it from the influence of the general government in most of what relates to its individuals of social rights. Thus, it was independent in command, prohibition, penalty, policy, and the like.

The master of the house was a deity for his family of wife, children, and slaves. He was the owner among them, and no one owned besides him as long as he was an individual of the house. He was the guardian over them, the manager of their affair by his absolute, effective choice among them. He used to worship the previous master of the house from his ancestors.

If there was property to be inherited by the household—such as when some of the sons died leaving behind what they had earned through labor with the permission of the head of the household (رب البيت), or some of the daughters died leaving behind what they possessed of dower (صداق) through marriage with the permission of the head of the household, or some of the relatives died—it was the head of the household alone who would inherit it. This was because it was the necessary implication of his lordship (ربوبيته) and his absolute ownership (ملكه المطلق) of the household and its members.

When the head of the household died, he would be inherited by one of his sons or brothers who had the capacity for that role. If the sons inherited and then separated to establish new households, they became the lords (أرباب) of those houses. If they remained in their old household, their relationship to the new lord (their brother, for example) was the same as their previous relationship to their father: falling under his guardianship (قيمومته) and absolute authority (ولايته المطلقة).

Similarly, adopted sons (الأدعياء) would inherit from him, as the practice of adoption (الادعاء والتبني) was prevalent among them, just as it was among the Arabs during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية).

As for women—such as the wife, the daughter, and the mother—they did not inherit, lest the property of the household be transferred, through their transition, to other households via marriage. They did not consider it permissible for wealth to move from one household to another. This is perhaps what some authors referred to when they said that these people believed in social-collective ownership (الملكية الاشتراكية الاجتماعية) rather than individual-private ownership (الانفرادية الفردية). However, I believe the basis for this is something other than collective ownership. Even primitive, nomadic peoples from the earliest times would prevent other nomadic tribes from sharing in the pastures and fertile lands they had acquired and protected for themselves; they would fight over them and defend their protected territories. This is a type of social-public ownership (الملك العام الاجتماعي) where the owner is the social collective (هيئة المجتمع) rather than its individuals. Nevertheless, this does not negate the fact that each individual in that society may possess something of this public property by way of exclusive right (اختصاصا).

This is a valid form of ownership (ملك صحيح الاعتبار), though they did not know how to regulate it properly or derive continuous benefit from it. Islam respected this principle, as we mentioned previously. The Exalted said: **هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا** — "It is He who created for you all that is in the earth" (Al-Baqarah: 29). Thus, the human society—specifically the Islamic society and those under its protection (ذمته)—is the owner of the earth's wealth in this sense. Furthermore, the Islamic society is the owner of the wealth in its possession; for this reason, Islam does not permit a disbeliever (كافر) to inherit from a Muslim.

Traces and examples of this perspective exist in some contemporary nations where they do not permit foreigners to own land or immovable property in their homelands, and so forth.

Since the household in ancient Rome was an independent and complete entity in itself, this ancient custom became firmly established there, just as it was in independent tribes and kingdoms.

The establishment of this custom or tradition (سنة) in Roman households, combined with their marriage laws which prohibited marrying close relatives (المحارم), resulted in kinship being divided into two types: first, natural kinship (القرابة الطبيعية), which is the sharing of blood; its implication was the prohibition of marriage with close relatives and its permissibility with others. Second, formal kinship (القرابة الرسمية), which is legal kinship; its implications were inheritance or its lack, maintenance (النفقة), guardianship (الولاية), and so on. Thus, sons were relatives possessing both natural and formal kinship in relation to the head of the household and among themselves. Women, however, were all possessors of natural kinship but not formal kinship; therefore, a woman would not inherit from her father, her child, her brother, her husband, or anyone else. This was the tradition of ancient Rome.

As for the Greeks, their ancient social structure regarding households was close to that of ancient Rome. Inheritance among them was granted to the eldest of the male children, while all women—wife, daughter, and sister—were deprived. Younger children and others were also deprived. However, like the Romans, they would sometimes resort to legal ruses to bequeath property to their younger sons or to those they loved and felt compassion for among their wives, daughters, and sisters, using various means to facilitate their enjoyment of some inheritance, whether little or much, through a will (وصية) or the like. Discussion regarding the matter of the will shall follow.

As for India, Egypt, and China, the matter of inheritance regarding the absolute deprivation of women and the deprivation of weak children—or their remaining under permanent guardianship and custody—was similar to the aforementioned traditions of the Romans and Greeks.

As for the Persians, they permitted marriage with close relatives (نكاح المحارم) and polygamy, as mentioned before, and they practiced adoption. The most beloved of wives to the husband would sometimes take the place of a son through adoption and would inherit equally with a son and an adopted son, while the rest of the wives were deprived. A married daughter would not inherit to prevent wealth from leaving the household, while the daughter who was not yet married would inherit half the share of a son. Thus, the non-senior wives and the married daughter were deprived, while the senior wife, the son, the adopted son, and the unmarried daughter were provided for.

As for the Arabs, they used to deprive women absolutely, as well as young boys. They granted inheritance only to the eldest of the children who could ride a horse and defend the honor (الحرمة); if there were none, then it went to the agnates (العصبة) — *the male relatives on the father's side*.

This was the state of the world when the verses of inheritance were revealed. This has been mentioned and addressed by many histories of national customs, travelogues, legal texts, and the like; whoever wishes to examine the details may refer to them.

In summary of all that has passed, the tradition (السنة) had become established in the world at that time upon the deprivation of women—in their capacity as wife, mother, daughter, or sister—except under other various titles, and upon the deprivation of children and orphans, except in some cases under the title of permanent and uninterrupted guardianship and custody (القيمومة).

**4 - What did Islam do in such circumstances?**: It has been mentioned repeatedly that Islam holds that the true foundation for human laws and regulations is the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — upon which people were created, and there is no change in Allah’s creation. It built inheritance upon the foundation of the womb (الرحم) — *blood relationship* — which is part of the fixed primordial nature and creation. It abolished the inheritance of adopted sons, as the Exalted says: **وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ ذَلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَاهِكُمْ وَاللَّهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ ادْعُوهُمْ لِآبَائِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ اللَّهِ فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوا آبَاءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَمَوَالِيكُمْ** — "nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons. These are mere utterances of your mouths. But Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way. Call them after their fathers; that is more just with Allah. If you do not know their fathers, then they are your brethren in faith and your wards" (Al-Ahzab: 4-5).

Then, it removed the will (الوصية) from the category of inheritance and made it an independent category through which property is given and taken, even if people call the acquisition of property through a bequest "inheritance." This is not merely a difference in naming, for both the will and inheritance have different rationales (ملاك) and independent primordial origins. The rationale for inheritance is the womb (الرحم), and the will of the deceased has no influence over it whatsoever. The rationale for the will is the effectiveness of the deceased's intent after his death (or, if you prefer, at the time he makes the bequest) regarding what he owned during his life, and the respect for his wish. If the will were included within inheritance, it would be nothing more than a name.

As for why people, such as those in ancient Rome, called it inheritance, it was not because they considered either the womb or the respect for the deceased's intent as the basis for the tradition of inheritance. Rather, the reality was that they built inheritance upon the respect for intent—specifically, the intent of the deceased for the inherited wealth to remain in the household where it was, under the hand of the head and lord of the house, or his intent for it to transfer after death to those whom the deceased loved and felt compassion for. Thus, inheritance in any case was built upon the respect for intent. Had it been built upon the principle of the womb and the sharing of blood, many of those deprived of wealth would have been provided for, and many of those provided for would have been deprived.

Furthermore, Islam then approached inheritance based on two fundamental principles:

The principle of the womb (أصل الرحم): This is the common element between a human and his relatives, in which males and females, adults and children—even fetuses in their mothers' wombs—do not differ, although its effect varies in terms of priority and posteriority, and the exclusion of some by others due to its strength or weakness based on proximity to or distance from the person, and the absence or presence of intermediaries, whether few or many, such as the child, the brother, and the uncle. This principle necessitates the entitlement to the essence of inheritance while maintaining the order of the preceding and succeeding classes (الطبقات).

The principle of the difference between male and female (أصل اختلاف الذكر والأنثى): This pertains to the nature of the dispositions arising from the difference in their equipment with intellect (التعقل) and emotions (الإحساسات). Man, by his nature, is a being of intellect, just as woman is the manifestation of emotions and delicate, tender feelings. This difference has a prominent impact on their lives regarding the management of owned property and its expenditure on needs. This principle is what necessitates the difference in shares between the man and the woman, even if they fall within the same class, such as the son and the daughter, or the brother and the sister in general, as we shall explain.

From the first principle, it derived the arrangement of classes (الطبقات) according to proximity to and distance from the deceased, based on the absence, fewness, or multiplicity of intermediaries. The first class is that which is related to the deceased without an intermediary: the son, the daughter, the father, and the mother. The second is the brother, the sister, the grandfather, and the grandmother; these are related to the deceased through one intermediary: the father, the mother, or both. The third is the paternal uncle and aunt, and the maternal uncle and aunt; these are related to the deceased through two intermediaries: the father or mother of the deceased, and his grandfather or grandmother. Following this logic, children in every class stand in the place of their parents and exclude the subsequent class. The state of the two spouses was taken into account because of the mixing of their blood through marriage with all classes; thus, no class excludes them, nor do they exclude any class.

Then, from the second principle, it derived the difference between male and female—except for the mother and the uterine relatives (الكلالة) related through the mother—in that the male receives the like of the portion of two females.

The six shares (السهام) prescribed in Islam—the half, the two-thirds, the one-third, the one-fourth, the one-sixth, and the one-eighth—though they vary, and likewise the wealth that eventually reaches one of the heirs even if it deviates from his prescribed share (فريضة) usually by way of redistribution (الرد) or the reduction (النقص) that occurs, and similarly the father, the mother, and the uterine relatives even if their prescribed shares deviate from the rule of "the male receives the like of the portion of two females"—for which reason a comprehensive universal study in the chapter of inheritance is difficult—nevertheless, all of it, considering the species in the succession of the predecessor by the successor, returns to the succession of one spouse by the other, and the succession of the generating class (the fathers and mothers) by the generated class (the children). The Islamic prescription in both groups—namely, spouses and children—is that the male receives the like of the portion of two females.

This universal view results in Islam seeing the division of existing wealth in the world into one-third and two-thirds: the female has one-third and the male has two-thirds. This is from the perspective of ownership (التملك). However, it does not hold a similar view regarding expenditure for needs (الصرف للحاجة); for it considers the maintenance (نفقة) of the wife to be the responsibility of the husband, commands justice which necessitates equality in consumption, and gives the woman independence of will and action in the property she owns, with no interference from the man. These three aspects result in the woman having the right to dispose of two-thirds of the world's wealth (the one-third she owns and half of the two-thirds owned by the man), while the man's disposal is only balanced against one-third.

**5 - What the status of women and orphans settled upon in Islam:** As for orphans, they inherit just like strong men. They are raised, and their wealth is grown under the guardianship of protectors (الأولياء), such as the father, the grandfather, the general body of believers, or the Islamic government, until they reach the age of marriage and maturity (الرشد) is perceived in them, at which point their wealth is handed over to them, and they attain a level of independent life. This is the most just of conceivable traditions regarding them.

As for women, according to the general view, they own one-third of the world's wealth and dispose of two-thirds of it, as per the previous explanation. They are free and independent in what they own, falling under neither permanent nor temporary guardianship. There is no blame upon men in what women do with themselves in an honorable manner (بالمعروف).

Thus, the woman in Islam possesses a personality equal to that of the man in freedom of will and action from all aspects. Her condition does not differ from that of the man except in what her specific spiritual attribute requires, which differs from the spiritual attribute of the man: she has an emotional life (حياة إحساسية), while the man's life is intellectual (تعقلية). Therefore, an increase in public ownership was considered for the man so that intellectual management in the world might prevail over emotional and sentimental management. The deficiency that befell her was compensated for by her dominance in disposal (تصرف). Obedience to the husband in the matter of intimacy was legislated upon her, and that was compensated for by the dower (الصداق). She was prohibited from the judiciary (القضاء), governance (الحكومة), and direct engagement in combat (المباشرة للقتال) because these are matters that must be built upon intellect rather than emotion. This was compensated for by the obligation upon men to protect them and defend their sanctity (حريمهن). The burdens of seeking livelihood and providing maintenance for her, the children, and the parents were placed upon the shoulders of men, while she has the right to the custody (حضانة) of children without it being an obligation. All these rulings were balanced by other matters to which they were called, such as the veil (التحجب), minimizing mixing with men, managing the home, and raising children.

The bitter results that human society tastes following the dominance of emotion over intellect in our present age have clarified the meaning of Islam's refusal to grant general social management—such as the management of defense, the judiciary, and governance—to sentiment and emotion, and placing their reins in its hand.
