By reflecting on the great world wars, which are among the "gifts" of modern civilization, and on the general conditions governing the world, and by presenting these events to the intellect and emotional sentiment, you will arrive at a diagnosis of what constitutes deception and what constitutes sincere advice. And Allah is the Guide.

Furthermore, the civilized Western nations have spared no effort and neglected no desire for hundreds of years in educating girls alongside boys in the same rank, bringing out their potential for perfection from potency to act. Yet, despite this, if you look at the list of political geniuses, men of the judiciary and legislation, and leaders and commanders of wars (which are the three aforementioned traits: governance, judiciary, and combat), you will not find any significant number of women's names, nor a number that can be compared to the hundreds and thousands of men. This in itself is the truest witness that the nature of women does not accept maturity and growth in these traits, in which, by nature, only intellect (التعقل) has authority. Whenever the creeping of emotions increases in them, failure and loss increase.

This and its like are among the most decisive answers to the famous theory which claims that the sole reason for women's lagging behind men in human society is the lack of proper education for them since the earliest eras of humanity; and that if proper and good education had continued for them, along with their delicate feelings and emotions, they would have caught up with men or surpassed them in aspects of perfection.

This reasoning is more akin to reasoning with what produces the opposite of the desired conclusion. For their specialization in delicate emotions, or the increase thereof in them, is precisely what necessitates their lagging behind in matters requiring the strength of intellect and its mastery over delicate spiritual emotions, such as governance and the judiciary, and the advancement of those who surpass them in that, namely men. Definitive experiments show that whoever is specialized in the strength of a spiritual attribute will only succeed in education regarding the purposes and needs that suit it. The necessary implication is that the education of men succeeds in matters like governance and the judiciary, and they excel over women in attaining perfection therein; and that the education of women succeeds in what suits delicate emotions and is linked to them, such as some branches of medicine, painting, music, weaving, cooking, raising children, nursing the sick, aspects of beautification, and the like. The two groups are equal in matters other than these.

Moreover, if their lagging behind in the mentioned matters were based on coincidence and chance as claimed, it would have been overturned in some of these long ages in which human society has lived—which they have estimated at millions of years—just as the lagging of men in matters specific to women would have been. If it were permissible for us to consider matters necessary to the species, inseparable from their society—especially if they suit internal matters in the human constitution—as coincidences, we would not be able to identify any natural, primordial trait (خلة طبيعية فطرية) among general human traits, such as the inclination of their nature toward civilization, their love for knowledge, their search for the secrets of events, and the like. For these are necessary attributes of this species, and in the constitution of its individuals, there are dispositions that suit them; we therefore consider them primordial attributes. Similarly, we consider the advancement of women in refined, aesthetic matters and their lagging in intellectual, formidable, and difficult matters as a requirement of their dispositions; and likewise, the advancement and lagging of men in the opposite.

Nothing remains after all this except their aversion to the attribution of the perfection of intellect to men and the perfection of emotion and compassion to themselves. This is misplaced, for intellect and emotion, in the view of Islam, are two divine gifts deposited in the human constitution for true divine purposes in life; neither has superiority over the other, and there is no nobility except through godwariness (التقوى). As for other perfections, whatever they may be, they only grow and thrive if they fall within its path; otherwise, they are counted as nothing but evil burdens.

**6 - Modern Inheritance Laws:** These laws and traditions, although they differ from the Islamic law of inheritance in quantity and quality—as will be summarized for you—nevertheless relied, in their emergence and establishment, on the Islamic tradition of inheritance. How great is the difference between the position of Islam at the time of legislating women's inheritance in the world and their current position.

Islam manifested something that the world did not know, nor had anything like it struck the ears of people, nor had later generations mentioned it from their past ancestors and early fathers. As for these modern laws, they were introduced and imposed upon nations when the Islamic tradition of inheritance had already become established among Islamic nations in most of the inhabited world among hundreds of millions of people, inherited by later generations from their ancestors for more than ten centuries. It is among the self-evident truths in psychological research that the occurrence of a matter in reality and then its stability and establishment is the best aid in the occurrence of what resembles it. Every preceding social tradition is intellectual material for subsequent similar traditions; rather, the first is the material that transforms into the second. Thus, no social researcher can deny the reliance of modern inheritance laws on the Islamic inheritance that preceded them, and its transformation into them, whether through a just or unjust transformation.

Among the strangest of statements is what is sometimes said—may Allah destroy the prejudice of the first Age of Ignorance—that modern laws only benefited in their articles from ancient Roman law. You have already learned what the ancient Roman tradition was regarding inheritance, and what the Islamic tradition offered to human society, and that the Islamic tradition is intermediate in emergence and practical application between ancient Roman laws and modern Western laws, and was known and deeply rooted in the society of millions and hundreds of millions of human souls for long, successive centuries. It is impossible for it to remain in vain and aside from influencing the thoughts of these legislators.

Even stranger is that these same speakers mention that Islamic inheritance is taken from ancient Roman inheritance!

In short, the modern laws prevalent among Western nations, though they differ in some particulars, are almost unanimous on the equality of men and women in the share of inheritance; thus, daughters and sons are equal, and mothers and fathers are equal in shares, and so on.

The classes in French law have been arranged in this manner: (1) Sons and daughters (2) Fathers, mothers, brothers, and sisters (3) Grandfathers and grandmothers (4) Paternal uncles and aunts, and maternal uncles and aunts. They have removed the marital bond from these classes and built it on the basis of love and heart-felt connection. We are not concerned with addressing the details of that or the details of the situation in other classes; whoever desires them may refer to their source.

What concerns us is reflecting on the result of this current tradition, which is the partnership of the woman with the man in the world's existing wealth according to the general view previously mentioned. However, they placed the wife under the guardianship (قيمومة) of the husband; she has no right to any financial disposal of any of her inherited property except with her husband's permission. Thus, the wealth returned to being halved between the man and the woman in ownership, but under the authority (ولاية) of the man in management and administration! There are activist associations exerting their efforts to give women independence and remove them from the guardianship of men regarding their property. If they succeed in what they desire, men and women would be equal in terms of ownership and in terms of the authority of management and disposal.

**7 - A Comparison of These Traditions with One Another:** After presenting a summary of the traditions prevalent among past nations and their bygone centuries to the critical researcher, we leave it to him to compare them with one another and to judge each of them in terms of completeness and deficiency, and its benefit or harm to human society in terms of its placement on the path of happiness; then to compare what the Legislator of Islam has ordained with them and to judge as must be judged.

The fundamental difference between the Islamic tradition and others lies in the end and purpose. The purpose of Islam is for the world to attain its rectification (صلاح), while the purpose of others is for it to attain what it desires (تشتهيها). From these two principles branch out the consequences. The Exalted said: **وَعَسَى أَن تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئًا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ وَعَسَى أَن تُحِبُّوا شَيْئًا وَهُوَ شَرٌّ لَّكُمْ وَاللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** — "It may be that you detest something, which is good for you, and it may be that you love something, which is bad for you, and Allah knows and you do not know" (Al-Baqarah: 216). And the Exalted said: **وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ فَإِن كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسَى أَن تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئًا وَيَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ فِيهِ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا** — "Consort with them in an honourable manner; and should you dislike them, maybe you dislike something while Allah invests it with much good" (An-Nisa: 19).

**8 - The Will:** It has been mentioned that Islam removed the will (الوصية) from the category of inheritance and made it an independent category because of its independent rationale (ملاك), which is the respect for the owner's intent regarding what he owned during his life. Among past nations, the will was one of the ways of deception to give the testator's wealth, or part of it, to someone other than those whom the current tradition dictated should inherit, such as the father or the head of the household. Therefore, they continued to enact laws to limit it and block this path that led to the nullification of the ruling of inheritance, and the matter continues to follow this course of limitation even today.

Islam limited it by its effectiveness up to one-third of the property; thus, it is not effective for anything exceeding that. Some modern laws, such as French law, have followed it in this, though the two perspectives differ. For this reason, Islam encourages it, while modern laws discourage it or remain silent.

What reflection on the verses of the will, charities (الصدقات), the poor-rate (الزكاة), the one-fifth (الخمس), and general spending (الإنفاق) yields is that these legislations facilitate the path for nearly half of the capital of wealth and two-thirds of its benefits to be placed toward good deeds, charities, and the needs of the class of the poor and the destitute. This is to bring the different classes in society closer together, to remove the distant gaps between them, and to support the destitute, alongside the laws enacted regarding how the wealthy dispose of their wealth to bring their class closer to the class of the destitute. The detail of this discussion has another place that you will encounter, Allah willing.
